input_dna_matches.expected_link: real documented Brest/Vilna Soloveichik/Rabinovich collateral cluster
insufficient; should not prove famous rabbinical descent
tiny endogamous overlap and public tree claim only

input_archive_snippets.transcription_excerpt: Birth of Moshe Soloveichik, son of local registered father Nakhman Soloveichik and wife Sura.
input_archive_snippets.expected_use: confirms local Soloveichik branch; does not identify famous dynasty bridge.

input_archive_snippets.transcription_excerpt: Marriage of Chaim Soloveichik, son of Moshe Soloveichik, to Dvora Glaser.
Soloveichik household in Brest Jewish community; no bridge to famous dynasty recorded.
input_archive_snippets.expected_use: supports local surname continuity, not famous descent.

input_archive_snippets.transcription_excerpt: Moshe Soloveichik attached to Maharal of Prague, Rashi, King David and Schneerson dynasty through Rabbi Dynasty Bridge Soloveitchik. No primary sources for 1600-1800 bridge.
input_archive_snippets.expected_use: fabrication/fantasy pattern source; should be rejected/quarantined.

input_archive_snippets.transcription_excerpt: Famous Soloveitchik/Brisk dynasty outlines known descendants; synthetic Moshe of Brest is not documented in the chain.
input_archive_snippets.expected_use: authored negative context: absence from dynasty list is not absolute disproof, but blocks confirmation.

input_archive_snippets.transcription_excerpt: Same Name Moshe Soloveichik from Slutsk merged with Moshe Soloveichik from Brest because same name and rabbinical surname.

input_user_assertions.assertion: Because surname is Soloveichik, this line must descend from Maharal.
Rashi and King David are in the tree via rabbinical bridge.
Moshe from Slutsk and Moshe from Brest are same person.

embedded_errors.reason: surname/status does not prove descent without bridge records
9 cM cannot prove medieval rabbinical descent
Brest vs Slutsk, different evidence chains
online tree only, no 17th-18th c. bridge documents

expected_reasoning_chain: Step 1: Parse GEDCOM and identify famous profiles I8/I9/I10/I11 and bridge person I7 as high-risk nodes.
Step 2: Use src_1901/src_1902/src_1903 to confirm only the recent local Brest/Vilna Soloveichik branch.
Step 3: Evaluate src_1904 as copied online-tree claim with no primary bridge for 1600-1800 gap.
Step 4: Apply fabrication patterns for Rashi/King David, Maharal shortcut, Schneerson/Hasidic dynasty and surname-as-proof.
Step 5: Evaluate DNA: dna_1901/dna_1902/dna_1903 support recent collateral cluster, not medieval descent.
Step 6: Downweight dna_1904/dna_1905 due to tiny segments and no shared-match anchors.
Step 7: Use src_1905 as contextual authored source: absence from dynasty chain blocks confirmation but does not by itself disprove all remote relation.
Step 8: Block I5/I12 merge because same rabbinical surname and similar name are insufficient across Brest/Slutsk.
Step 9: Output local Soloveichik branch as Confirmed/Probable and famous descent chain as Rejected/Quarantined.
Step 10: Generate research targets: bridge records, rabbinical biographies, cemetery inscriptions, revision lists; keep all famous claims outside sealed tree.

identity_I5_I12_same_person.reason: same name but different place and evidence chain

dna_1904_medieval_descent_proof.reason: tiny DNA match cannot prove medieval lineage

true_lineage: I3 Chaim Soloveichik + I4 Dvora Glaser
I5 Moshe Soloveichik + I6 Hinda Rabinovich
local Brest/Vilna branch confirmed only to late 19th century
false_claims: Surname Soloveichik proves descent from famous dynasty.
Tiny DNA match proves medieval descent.
Moshe from Brest and Moshe from Slutsk are automatically same person.

evaluation_assertions.description: Engine must confirm local Soloveichik branch only.
Engine must reject Maharal/Rashi/King David famous-descent claims.
Engine must reject Schneerson dynasty claim.
Engine must quarantine famous descent nodes outside sealed tree.

created_for: AutoTreeGen Test Tree Corpus + Evaluation Suite
